title_en: Major Shafi'i Scholars and Their Contributions
content_en:
Through its ten volumes, Tabaqat ash-Shafi'iyyah al-Kubra documents the lives and contributions of hundreds of Shafi'i scholars from ash-Shafi'i's immediate students through the Mamluk period in which as-Subki himself lived. The biographies of major scholars in this tradition reveal the intellectual and institutional development of the Shafi'i school across several centuries.

Among ash-Shafi'i's immediate students, the biographies of al-Muzani, ar-Rabi' ibn Sulayman al-Muraddi, and others document how the school's positions were first transmitted and how the divergence between the Old Egyptian School (the positions ash-Shafi'i had held while in Iraq) and the New Egyptian School (the positions he revised and refined in Egypt) was handled by his students. This historical detail about the school's earliest transmission is available primarily through the tabaqat literature.

The biography of Abu Ishaq ash-Shirazi (393–476 AH), the author of Al-Muhadhdhab and one of the great Shafi'i jurists of the fifth century, illustrates the kind of extended treatment that major scholars receive. As-Subki documents ash-Shirazi's training, his teachers, his relationship with al-Juwayni, his role at the Baghdad Nizamiyyah, the works he produced, the students he trained, and the controversies he navigated. The biography brings to life the scholarly culture of the Nizamiyyah period and the interconnection of legal and theological scholarship.

The biography of al-Ghazali is also detailed and reflective, addressing the complexity of his relationship to the Shafi'i school — he was technically a Shafi'i jurist but his most famous works transcended legal school boundaries — and his intellectual and spiritual journey from kalam theology to Sufi synthesis.

The biographies of Egyptian Shafi'i scholars from the Ayyubid and Mamluk periods document the school's institutional presence in Cairo, where it was one of the dominant legal traditions and where major Shafi'i scholars taught at al-Azhar and other institutions. This historical documentation of the school's Egyptian development is one of the work's distinctive contributions.
title_id: Ulama-ulama Syafi'i Besar dan Kontribusi Mereka
content_id:
Melalui sepuluh jilidnya, Thabaqat asy-Syafi'iyyah al-Kubra mendokumentasikan kehidupan dan kontribusi ratusan ulama Syafi'i dari murid-murid langsung asy-Syafi'i hingga periode Mamluk di mana as-Subki sendiri hidup. Biografi-biografi ulama besar dalam tradisi ini mengungkap perkembangan intelektual dan kelembagaan mazhab Syafi'i selama beberapa abad.

Di antara murid-murid langsung asy-Syafi'i, biografi-biografi al-Muzani, ar-Rabi' ibn Sulayman al-Muradi, dan lainnya mendokumentasikan bagaimana posisi-posisi mazhab pertama kali ditransmisikan dan bagaimana perbedaan antara Mazhab Mesir Lama (posisi-posisi yang dipegang asy-Syafi'i ketika di Irak) dan Mazhab Mesir Baru (posisi-posisi yang beliau revisi dan sempurnakan di Mesir) ditangani oleh murid-murid beliau. Detail sejarah tentang transmisi paling awal mazhab ini tersedia terutama melalui literatur thabaqat.

Biografi Abu Ishaq asy-Syirazi (393–476 H), pengarang Al-Muhadzdab dan salah satu ahli hukum Syafi'i besar abad ke-5, menggambarkan jenis pembahasan panjang yang diterima oleh ulama-ulama besar. As-Subki mendokumentasikan pelatihan asy-Syirazi, guru-gurunya, hubungannya dengan al-Juwaini, perannya di Nizhamiyyah Baghdad, karya-karya yang ia hasilkan, murid-murid yang ia latih, dan kontroversi-kontroversi yang ia navigasi.

Biografi al-Ghazali juga terperinci dan reflektif, membahas kompleksitas hubungannya dengan mazhab Syafi'i — beliau secara teknis adalah ahli hukum Syafi'i tetapi karya-karyanya yang paling terkenal melampaui batas-batas mazhab hukum — dan perjalanan intelektual dan spiritualnya dari teologi kalam ke sintesis Sufi.

Biografi-biografi ulama Syafi'i Mesir dari periode Ayyubiyah dan Mamluk mendokumentasikan kehadiran kelembagaan mazhab di Kairo, di mana ia adalah salah satu tradisi hukum yang dominan dan di mana ulama-ulama Syafi'i besar mengajar di al-Azhar dan lembaga-lembaga lainnya.
content_ar: Through its ten volumes, Tabaqat ash-Shafi'iyyah al-Kubra documents the lives and contributions of hundreds of الشافعي العلماء from ash-الشافعي's immediate students through the Mamluk period in which as-Subki himself lived. The biographies of major العلماء in this التراث reveal the intellectual and institutional development of the الشافعي school across several centuries. Among ash-الشافعي's immediate students, the biographies of al-Muzani, ar-Rabi' ibn سليمان ﷺ al-Muraddi, and others document how the school's positions were أولاً transmitted and how the divergence between the Old Egyptian School (the positions ash-الشافعي had held بينما in Iraq) and the New Egyptian School (the positions he revised and refined in Egypt) was handled by his students. This historical detail about the school's earliest transmission is available primarily through the tabaqat literature. The biography of Abu Ishaq ash-Shirazi (393–476 AH), the author of Al-Muhadhdhab and one of the great الشافعي jurists of the خامساً century, illustrates the kind of extended treatment that major العلماء receive. As-Subki documents ash-Shirazi's training, his teachers, his relationship with al-Juwayni, his role at the Baghdad Nizamiyyah, the works he produced, the students he trained, and the controversies he navigated. The biography brings to الحياة the العلمي culture of the Nizamiyyah period and the interconnection of legal and theological scholarship. The biography of al-Ghazali is أيضاً detailed and reflective, addressing the complexity of his relationship to the الشافعي school — he was technically a الشافعي jurist but his most famous works transcended legal school boundaries — and his intellectual and spiritual journey from علم الكلام theology to Sufi synthesis. The biographies of Egyptian الشافعي العلماء from the Ayyubid and Mamluk periods document the school's institutional presence in Cairo, where it was one of the dominant legal traditions and where major الشافعي العلماء taught at al-Azhar and other institutions. This historical documentation of the school's Egyptian development is one of the work's distinctive contributions.
